title: Number of farmed crustaceans killed for food
subtitle: The data includes farmed crustaceans such as shrimps, crabs, lobsters, prawns, and crayfish.
caption:
Number of farmed crustaceans killed for food

The data includes farmed crustaceans such as shrimps, crabs, lobsters, prawns, and crayfish.

This bar chart presents estimates for the number of farmed crustaceans killed for food in 2022, broken down by the World total and three specific countries: China, Indonesia, and Ecuador. The data is displayed using three distinct bars for each entity to represent the range of uncertainty: a grey bar for the upper bound, a red bar for the mid-point estimate, and a grey bar for the lower bound. The note clarifies that these bounds exist due to large uncertainties in the data, with the mid-point values calculated as the average of the upper and lower limits. The World total shows the largest magnitude, with the mid-point estimate at 630 billion, bounded by a lower value of 310 billion and an upper value of 950 billion. China follows as the largest single country shown, with a mid-point estimate of 320 billion, ranging from a lower bound of 190 billion to an upper bound of 450 billion. The values for Indonesia and Ecuador are significantly lower in comparison. Indonesia displays a mid-point estimate of 49 billion, with bounds spanning from 20 billion to 77 billion. Ecuador shows a mid-point estimate of 62 billion, with a lower bound of 22 billion and an upper bound of 100 billion. The visual gap between the upper and lower bounds is substantial for all categories, illustrating the wide margin of error in the global counts. The red mid-point bars consistently sit visually centered between the grey bound bars across all four groups. The data shows that the global total is roughly double the estimate for China alone, while the estimates for Indonesia and Ecuador are an order of magnitude smaller than the Chinese figures.
